schools.summary: Augustine: the connection with the foregoing is that 'he who loveth God, love his brother also,' for the begetting love runs from the Father to all who are begotten of him, so that one cannot love the Father without loving the children he has begotten; John declaring that whoever believes Jesus is the Christ is born of God, and everyone who loves the Father loves also him that is begotten of him — the new birth shown by faith, and love of God issuing in love of the brethren (John joining faith and love as the twin marks of the regenerate: 'whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God,' true saving faith in Jesus as the Christ being the fruit and evidence of the new birth, for none can so believe but the regenerate, 'and every one that loveth him that begat loveth him also that is begotten of him'; the family argument, that love to 'him that begat,' the Father, necessarily flows out to 'him also that is begotten of him,' every child of God, the brethren, so he who truly loves the Father will love all the Father's children, faith and brotherly love being inseparable in the born-again soul).

themes: whosoever believeth that Jesus is the Christ is born of God
every one that loveth him that begat loveth him also that is begotten of him
faith and love the twin marks of the regenerate
love of the Father flowing to all his children

schools.summary: Gill: the apostle's view is to show when love to the saints is right, namely when we love God and keep his commandments (the Ethiopic reading 'by this know that we love God,' which would make a tautology, proving idem per idem); John declaring that by this we know we love the children of God, when we love God and keep his commandments — the test of true brotherly love in the love of God and obedience to him (John, having said love of God flows to his children, now turning it about to give the test: 'by this we know that we love the children of God, when we love God, and keep his commandments'; the mutual proof, that love to God's children is proved genuine when joined to love of God and obedience, and love of God is proved real by love to his children, so the two loves and obedience all hang together, each testing and confirming the other, true love to the brethren being not a mere natural fondness but springing from love to God and showing itself in keeping his commandments).

themes: by this we know that we love the children of God
when we love God, and keep his commandments
the test of true brotherly love
love to the brethren springing from love to God and obedience

schools.summary: Gill: keeping of the commandments of God is an evidence of love to God; this shows that love is not in word and tongue, in profession only, but in deed and in truth, and that such persons have a sense of the love of God upon their souls, under the influence of which they act; John declaring that this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments, and his commandments are not grievous — the proof of love in obedience, and the lightness of God's commands to the believer (John defining love of God in practical terms: 'for this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments,' the true love of God showing itself not in mere profession but in obedience, the keeping of his commandments being the very proof and fruit of love, and adding a sweet encouragement: 'and his commandments are not grievous'; the comfort, that to the believer, made willing and given a new nature, God's commandments are 'not grievous,' not heavy, burdensome, or irksome as they are to the unwilling, but, being kept in love and by the help of grace, are light and even delightful, so love makes obedience, and obedience love makes easy).

themes: for this is the love of God, that we keep his commandments
and his commandments are not grievous
love of God proved in obedience
God's commands light and delightful to the willing believer

schools.summary: Gill: 'whatsoever is born of God' may be understood either of persons born of God, or of the new creature or principle of grace wrought in them, particularly faith hereafter mentioned, which is a heaven-born grace, the gift of God, and the operation of his Spirit; John declaring that whatsoever is born of God overcomes the world, and this is the victory that overcomes the world, even our faith — the victory over the world won by the faith of the new-born soul (John giving the reason why God's commands are not grievous, that the regenerate has a conquering power: 'for whatsoever is born of God overcometh the world,' every one born of God, and the new principle of grace in him, gaining the victory over the world, its frowns and flatteries, its sins and snares, and naming the weapon of this conquest: 'and this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith'; it being faith, the heaven-born grace, that overcomes, faith laying hold of Christ and the unseen world by which the believer rises above and triumphs over the world that would master others, so the new birth makes overcomers, and faith is the victory).

themes: for whatsoever is born of God overcometh the world
this is the victory that overcometh the world, even our faith
the victory over the world won by the new-born soul
faith laying hold of Christ, triumphing over the world

schools.summary: Gill: this question carries in it a strong affirmation, that no other person is the conqueror of the world; Barnes: where is there one who can pretend to have obtained a victory over the world, except he who believes in the Saviour? All else are worldly and are governed by the world; John asking who overcomes the world but he that believes Jesus is the Son of God — the overcomer identified as the believer in Christ the Son of God (John, by a pointed question, fixing the identity of the world-conqueror: 'who is he that overcometh the world, but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God?'; the force of the question, that it is a strong way of affirming there is no other overcomer at all, none but the one who truly believes that Jesus is 'the Son of God' gaining the victory, all others, however strong or wise, being mastered by the world and living under its power, but the believer, his faith fixed on the divine Son, rising above it and conquering, so the world-overcoming faith is faith in the deity and Sonship of Jesus).

themes: who is he that overcometh the world
but he that believeth that Jesus is the Son of God
the overcomer identified as the believer in Christ
faith in the Son of God the world-overcoming faith

schools.summary: Gill: by 'water' is not meant the ablutions or washings of the ceremonial law (Christ came to make an end of them), nor only the purity of his nature, life, and conversation, but rather his baptism and the cleansing of the Spirit, and by 'blood' his atoning death, which cleanses from all sin; John declaring that this is he that came by water and blood, even Jesus Christ, not by water only but by water and blood, and the Spirit bears witness because the Spirit is truth — Christ's coming by water and blood and the Spirit's witness to it (John setting out the witnesses to Christ's true Sonship and saving work: 'this is he that came by water and blood, even Jesus Christ; not by water only, but by water and blood'; the debate on the meaning, some taking them of Christ's baptism, water, and his atoning death, blood, others of the two streams water and blood that flowed from his pierced side, others of the cleansing and the atoning work of Christ or the two sacraments, but all agreeing that Christ came not by one only but by both, both his cleansing and his atoning being needful, 'and it is the Spirit that beareth witness, because the Spirit is truth,' the Holy Spirit adding his own sure testimony being himself the truth).

debates.question: What is meant by Christ coming 'by water and blood'?

sides.position: His baptism (water) and his atoning death (blood) — the two marking his ministry from start to finish
The two streams, water and blood, that flowed from his pierced side
His cleansing/sanctifying work and his atoning work — or the two sacraments that signify them

themes: this is he that came by water and blood, even Jesus Christ
not by water only, but by water and blood
it is the Spirit that beareth witness, because the Spirit is truth
Christ's coming confirmed by the Spirit of truth

schools.summary: Gill: the genuineness of this text has been called in question by some, because it is wanting in the Syriac version, as also in the Arabic and Ethiopic versions, and the old Latin interpreter has it not, and it is not found in many Greek copies; yet it is in many Greek manuscripts and cited by ancient writers (Tertullian, Cyprian), and the sense agrees with the doctrine of the Trinity; John declaring that there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost, and these three are one — the threefold heavenly witness of the Trinity, with debate over the verse's genuineness (John setting forth the highest witness to the Sonship of Christ: 'for there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost'; the great textual debate, that the genuineness of this clause, the 'Johannine Comma,' is disputed, for it is absent from the Syriac, Arabic, Ethiopic, old Latin, and many Greek manuscripts though found in others and cited by some ancients, yet the doctrine it teaches being firmly grounded elsewhere in Scripture, the three Persons of the Godhead bearing united witness, and 'these three are one,' one in nature, essence, and testimony, a clear statement of the unity of the Trinity).

debates.question: Is the heavenly-witness clause (the 'Johannine Comma') genuine?

sides.position: Its genuineness is doubted — it is absent from the Syriac, Arabic, Ethiopic, old Latin, and many Greek copies, though found in some manuscripts and cited by certain ancients
Whatever the textual question, the doctrine it teaches — the Trinity, three Persons who are one — is firmly grounded elsewhere in Scripture

themes: for there are three that bear record in heaven, the Father, the Word, and the Holy Ghost
and these three are one
the threefold heavenly witness of the Trinity
the disputed genuineness of the Johannine Comma

schools.summary: Gill: these three bear witness on earth to the same truth of the sonship of Christ; Barnes notes the genuine passage (omitting the disputed heavenly clause) reads 'there are three that bear record, the Spirit, and the water, and the blood,' continuing their testimony; John declaring that there are three that bear witness on earth, the Spirit, the water, and the blood, and these three agree in one — the threefold earthly witness uniting in testimony to Christ (John naming a second set of witnesses, on earth, to the truth of Christ's Sonship and saving work: 'there are three that bear witness in earth, the Spirit, and the water, and the blood: and these three agree in one'; the harmony, that the 'Spirit,' the Holy Spirit's testimony in the word and in the hearts of believers, the 'water,' Christ's baptism and cleansing, and the 'blood,' his atoning death, all bear one consistent witness, and 'these three agree in one,' concurring in a single testimony that Jesus is the Son of God and the Saviour, so earth as well as heaven has its threefold witness, all uniting to confirm the believer's faith in Christ).

themes: and there are three that bear witness in earth, the Spirit, and the water, and the blood
and these three agree in one
the threefold earthly witness uniting to Christ
earth as well as heaven bearing witness

schools.summary: Gill: the witness of a sufficient number of credible men, of men of good character and report, is always admitted in any case and in any court of judicature; it was allowed of in the law of Moses, everything being proved and established hereby, and upon this men were justified or condemned, so much more is the witness of God to be received; John reasoning that, if we receive the witness of men, the witness of God is greater, this being the witness God testified of his Son — the surpassing weight of God's own witness to his Son (John drawing the argument from the lesser to the greater: 'if we receive the witness of men,' as we daily do, accepting the testimony of credible witnesses in every matter of life and law, 'the witness of God is greater,' how much more should we receive the testimony of God himself, of infinitely greater weight and certainty, 'for this is the witness of God which he hath testified of his Son'; the point, that the witnesses just named, Spirit, water, blood, and the heavenly Three, are God's own testimony to his Son, and if men readily believe one another, it is the height of unbelief and folly to refuse the witness of the God who cannot lie, given concerning his Son).

themes: if we receive the witness of men, the witness of God is greater
for this is the witness of God which he hath testified of his Son
God's witness surpassing all human testimony
to refuse it the height of unbelief

schools.summary: Gill: he that believes on the Son of God, as a divine person who came in the flesh and obeyed the law and brought in everlasting righteousness and obtained life and salvation for men, he that with the heart believes in him for righteousness and eternal life, being the Son of God, truly and properly God and so able to save, has the witness in himself; John declaring that he that believes on the Son of God has the witness in himself, but he that believes not has made God a liar, not believing the record of his Son — the inward witness of faith and the great guilt of refusing God's testimony (John showing the two opposite states: 'he that believeth on the Son of God hath the witness in himself,' the believer having an inward, experimental witness, the Spirit's testimony in his heart, the assurance and evidence of the truth he believes written within him, while 'he that believeth not God hath made him a liar; because he believeth not the record that God gave of his Son'; the dreadful charge against unbelief, that to refuse God's testimony concerning his Son is in effect to call God a liar, charging the God of truth with falsehood, so the believer carries the witness within but the unbeliever, by his very unbelief, commits the awful sin of giving God the lie).

themes: he that believeth on the Son of God hath the witness in himself
he that believeth not God hath made him a liar
because he believeth not the record that God gave of his Son
the inward witness of faith, the guilt of unbelief

schools.summary: Barnes: this is the sum, or the amount, of the testimony which God has given respecting his Son; Gill: the sum and substance of the record, with respect to the person of Christ and the security of salvation in him, who is the true God and eternal life; John declaring that this is the record, that God has given us eternal life, and this life is in his Son — the substance of God's testimony, eternal life freely given and treasured in Christ (John giving the very heart and content of God's witness: 'and this is the record, that God hath given to us eternal life, and this life is in his Son'; the two great truths, first 'God hath given to us eternal life,' eternal life being God's free gift, not earned but bestowed on his people in his love, and second 'this life is in his Son,' the life being treasured up and to be found in Christ alone, not apart from him, so the whole testimony of God comes to this gracious sum: eternal life is the gift of God, and it is laid up in his Son, so that to have the Son is to have life).

themes: and this is the record, that God hath given to us eternal life
and this life is in his Son
the substance of God's testimony
eternal life God's gift, treasured in Christ

schools.summary: Gill: he that hath the Son has a spiritual and experimental knowledge of him, true faith in him, who has him dwelling in his heart and living in him; Barnes: John evidently designs to refer to the principle laid down by the Saviour (John 5:24), that he that has the Son has life; John declaring that he that hath the Son hath life, and he that hath not the Son of God hath not life — the having of the Son the dividing line between life and death (John drawing the sharp dividing conclusion from the record: since the life is in the Son, it follows plainly that 'he that hath the Son hath life,' the one who has Christ, by faith possessing, knowing, and being united to him, having eternal life, for the life is in Christ and so in all who have him, 'and he that hath not the Son of God hath not life'; the absoluteness of the statement, that there is no middle ground, to have the Son being to have life, to be without the Son being to be without life however else a man may seem to live, so everything turns on the one question, whether a man has the Son of God).

themes: he that hath the Son hath life
and he that hath not the Son of God hath not life
the having of the Son the dividing line between life and death
everything turning on whether a man has the Son

schools.summary: Gill: 'these things have I written unto you' — what is contained in the epistle in general, and particularly what is written in the context concerning the victory over the world being ascribed to him who believes that Christ is the Son of God, and concerning the witnesses of his sonship and the record God bore; John declaring that he wrote these things to believers that they may know they have eternal life and believe on the name of the Son of God — the assuring aim of the letter, that believers may know they have eternal life (John stating the great purpose of his writing: 'these things have I written unto you that believe on the name of the Son of God; that ye may know that ye have eternal life'; the gracious end, that John writes not to the unbelieving but 'to you that believe,' and his aim is their assurance, 'that ye may know that ye have eternal life,' that believers might have not a doubtful hope but a settled knowledge and assurance of their present possession of eternal life, 'and that ye may believe on the name of the Son of God,' and so be confirmed and grow in their faith, so the whole epistle is given that believers may be sure they have life and may believe the more firmly).

themes: these things have I written unto you that believe on the name of the Son of God
that ye may know that ye have eternal life
the assuring aim of the letter
settled assurance of present possession of eternal life

schools.summary: Gill: 'the confidence that we have in him' is either in God to whom prayer is made, or in the Son of God through whose blood and righteousness believers in him have confidence with God at the throne of grace; they can come with boldness and intrepidity and use freedom and liberty of speech, as the word here used signifies; John declaring that this is the confidence we have in him, that if we ask anything according to his will, he hears us — the boldness in prayer when asking according to God's will (John adding another privilege flowing from the believer's standing: 'and this is the confidence that we have in him, that, if we ask any thing according to his will, he heareth us'; the ground and the condition of this confidence, that the believer may come to God in prayer with 'confidence,' boldness, freedom, assurance, not as a stranger but as a child sure of being heard, and the condition that makes prayer sure is that we ask 'according to his will,' in agreement with what God has revealed and purposed, in submission to his wisdom, for such asking God always 'heareth,' so prayer offered in faith and conformed to God's will has the certain promise of his attentive hearing).

themes: and this is the confidence that we have in him
that, if we ask any thing according to his will, he heareth us
the believer's boldness in prayer
sure of being heard when asking according to God's will

schools.summary: Gill: if we may be assured he does hear and answer all such persons that ask according to his will; Barnes: if we are assured of this as a true doctrine, then, even though we may not see immediately that the prayer is answered, we may have the utmost confidence that it will be; John reasoning that if we know he hears us, whatsoever we ask, we know we have the petitions we desired of him — the certainty of answered prayer that follows from God's hearing (John drawing the comforting inference from the confidence of v.14: 'and if we know that he hear us, whatsoever we ask,' since we are assured that God hears prayer offered according to his will, 'we know that we have the petitions that we desired of him'; the chain of assurance, that to know that God hears is to know that we have, for the God who hears the prayer of faith will surely grant it, so the believer may reckon the thing asked as already his own even before he sees it given, and, though the answer may be delayed or come in God's own way, the praying believer has the petitions as good as in hand, resting on the faithfulness of the God who hears).

themes: and if we know that he hear us, whatsoever we ask
we know that we have the petitions that we desired of him
the certainty of answered prayer
reckoning the thing asked as already his own

schools.summary: Gill: those who have such an interest at the throne of grace and such boldness there should make use of it for others as well as themselves, particularly for fallen believers, for a 'brother' not in a natural or civil but a spiritual sense, one judged to be born again; John charging that if any see his brother sin a sin not unto death, he shall ask and God shall give him life, but there is a sin unto death for which he does not bid him pray — the intercession for the erring brother, and the much-debated sin unto death (John applying the confidence in prayer to intercession for others: 'if any man see his brother sin a sin which is not unto death, he shall ask, and he shall give him life for them that sin not unto death,' the believer, seeing a brother fall into sin 'not unto death,' a sin not of the deadliest fatal kind, being to pray for him and God granting the erring one recovery and life, but John adding a solemn exception: 'there is a sin unto death: I do not say that he shall pray for it'; the great debate over the 'sin unto death,' some taking it of a sin punished with bodily death, others of apostasy or the unpardonable sin against the Holy Ghost, others of a course of sin that ends in death, but all agreeing John bids the believer pray for the recoverable while not commanding prayer for the one whose sin is unto death).

sides.position: A sin punished with bodily/temporal death
Apostasy or the unpardonable sin against the Holy Ghost — a final, irrecoverable rejection
A persistent course of sin that ends in spiritual death

themes: if any man see his brother sin a sin which is not unto death, he shall ask
he shall give him life for them that sin not unto death
there is a sin unto death: I do not say that he shall pray for it
intercession for the erring brother, with the debated exception

schools.summary: Gill: all unrighteousness against God or man is a sin against the law of God, and the wrath of God is revealed against it, and it is deserving of death; yet all unrighteousness is not unto death, as the sins of David, which were unrighteousness both to God and man and yet were put away, and he did not die; John declaring that all unrighteousness is sin, and there is a sin not unto death — the distinction between sin in general and the sin not unto death (John, lest any think some unrighteousness is no sin, laying down the general truth and the needful distinction: 'all unrighteousness is sin,' every act of unrighteousness against God or man being truly sin, a transgression of God's law deserving his wrath, 'and there is a sin not unto death'; the balance, that on the one hand John does not make light of any sin, for all unrighteousness is sin and in its nature deserves death, yet on the other he distinguishes, 'there is a sin not unto death,' sins into which even true believers fall, as David's, which through grace and the blood of Christ are forgiven and do not bring final ruin, so all sin is real and grievous but not all sin is the deadly, unrecoverable sin).

themes: all unrighteousness is sin
and there is a sin not unto death
all unrighteousness truly sin, yet not all unto death
sins believers fall into, forgiven through grace

schools.summary: Gill: whosoever is born of God, who is regenerated by his Spirit and grace, quickened by his power, has Christ formed in him, is made a partaker of the divine nature, and has every grace implanted, sinneth not; Matthew Henry: here we have a recapitulation of the privileges and advantages of sound Christian believers; John declaring that whosoever is born of God sinneth not, but he that is begotten of God keeps himself, and the wicked one touches him not — the kept and guarded life of the regenerate, not under sin's dominion nor Satan's grip (John affirming a great privilege of the new birth: 'we know that whosoever is born of God sinneth not'; the famous tension with John's earlier word that he who says he has no sin deceives himself, the meaning being not that the born-again never commit any sin at all but that the regenerate does not sin as the dominant course of his life, does not live in sin or practise it habitually, for the principle of grace in him resists it, 'but he that is begotten of God keepeth himself,' he watching and guarding himself, kept also by God, 'and that wicked one toucheth him not,' Satan cannot fatally grasp or destroy him, so the child of God is preserved from the reign of sin and the deadly touch of the evil one).

debates.question: In what sense does the one born of God 'sin not'?

sides.position: Not that the regenerate never commits any sin at all (for 1 John 1:8 says otherwise), but that he does not sin as the dominant course of his life, does not live in or habitually practise sin, the principle of grace resisting it

themes: we know that whosoever is born of God sinneth not
he that is begotten of God keepeth himself
and that wicked one toucheth him not
the regenerate kept from sin's reign and Satan's grip

schools.summary: Gill: 'we know that we are of God,' the sons of God and regenerated by him; this is known by the Spirit of God which witnesses to the spirits of the saints that they are the children of God, and by the fruits and effects of regenerating grace, as love to the brethren and the like; John declaring that we know we are of God, and the whole world lies in wickedness — the believer's assured standing in God set against the whole world lying in evil (John setting the believer's blessed certainty against the dark state of the world: 'and we know that we are of God,' believers knowing, by the Spirit's witness and the fruits of grace, that they belong to God, are his children, on his side, 'and the whole world lieth in wickedness'; the sharp contrast, that over against the people of God stands 'the whole world,' not a part but the whole mass of the unregenerate, which 'lieth in wickedness,' lies sunk and steeped in evil, and as some render lies 'in the wicked one,' under the power of Satan, so there are but two great companies, those who are 'of God' and the whole world lying in the grip of evil, and the believer knows to which he belongs).

themes: and we know that we are of God
and the whole world lieth in wickedness
the believer's assured standing against the world in evil
two companies: those of God, and the world under Satan

schools.summary: Gill: the Son of God is come, the second Person in the Godhead, equal to the Father and of the same nature, come from the Father from heaven into this world, not by local motion but by assumption of nature, come in the flesh or become incarnate to work out salvation; John declaring that the Son of God is come and has given us understanding to know him that is true, and we are in him that is true, even in his Son Jesus Christ, this is the true God and eternal life — the incarnate Son giving us to know and be in the true God, who is Christ himself (John gathering up the great certainties: 'we know that the Son of God is come,' truly incarnate, 'and hath given us an understanding, that we may know him that is true,' a spiritual understanding to know the true God, 'and we are in him that is true, even in his Son Jesus Christ,' and crowning it: 'this is the true God, and eternal life'; the weighty witness to Christ's deity, that the most natural reading takes 'this is the true God, and eternal life' of the nearest antecedent, 'his Son Jesus Christ,' so that Christ himself is here expressly called 'the true God' and 'eternal life,' a clear testimony to his full divinity, so the believer, brought by the incarnate Son to know and be in the true God, finds that Son to be himself the true God and the life eternal).

debates.question: Whom does 'This is the true God, and eternal life' name?

sides.position: Jesus Christ — the nearest antecedent ('his Son Jesus Christ'), so that Christ himself is expressly called 'the true God' and 'eternal life,' a clear witness to his deity

themes: we know that the Son of God is come, and hath given us an understanding
that we may know him that is true... even in his Son Jesus Christ
this is the true God, and eternal life
Christ himself named the true God and eternal life

schools.summary: Gill: 'keep yourselves from idols' — from heathen idols and idolatry, into which the saints in those times might be liable to be drawn by reason of their dwelling among heathen idolaters, being related to them, and the too great freedom used in eating things sacrificed to idols in their temples; John closing the epistle with the charge, 'Little children, keep yourselves from idols. Amen' — the tender final warning to guard against all idolatry (John ending his letter with a short, affectionate, and pointed exhortation: 'little children,' the tender name he loves to use, 'keep yourselves from idols. Amen'; the fitness of this close, that having shown them the true God, who is found in his Son Jesus Christ, John warns them to keep clear of all false gods, the believers, living among heathen idolaters, being in danger of being drawn into idolatry by kinship and custom and so charged to guard themselves; 'idols' may reach beyond carved images to whatever a man sets in God's place in his heart, so the warning is to keep the heart for the true God alone).

themes: little children, keep yourselves from idols. Amen
the tender final charge against idolatry
guarding against all false gods
keeping the heart for the true God alone
